title_en: Zakat and Fasting Disputes in Al-Insaf
title_ar: مسائل الخلاف في الزكاة والصيام في الإنصاف
content_en:
Al-Mardawi's treatment of zakat and fasting disputes in Al-Insaf demonstrates the depth of internal scholarly discussion within the Hanbali school on these pillars of Islam. Questions that might seem settled in other schools often reveal multiple transmitted opinions from Imam Ahmad himself, generating ongoing scholarly debates within the Hanbali tradition.

On whether zakat is obligatory on children and the insane, the Hanbali school has two major positions. The dominant (rajih) view — which al-Mardawi affirms — holds that zakat is obligatory on their wealth, with the guardian responsible for paying it. This is based on the general nature of the zakat obligation, which applies to the wealth rather than the person. The second Hanbali view holds that zakat requires sound legal capacity (taklif), which excludes children and the insane.

The question of zakat on debts generates complex discussion. If a person is owed a debt that is likely to be recovered, is zakat due on that debt during the years it is outstanding? The Hanbali school's positions range from: pay zakat each year even on outstanding debts (the strict position), to pay all accumulated years' zakat when the debt is recovered (the moderate position), to no zakat on debts that are uncertain of recovery (the lenient position). Al-Mardawi evaluates the evidential basis for each and identifies the rajih.

For Ramadan fasting, a disputed question concerns the person who breaks their fast believing it to be nighttime, then discovers the sun had not yet set. The Hanbali school is more lenient than some other schools in this scenario — one transmitted position holds the fast is valid if the person was genuinely mistaken and acted in good faith. Al-Mardawi presents the evidential debate and evaluates whether the fast must be made up or is valid.

The question of whether a traveler who begins a fast at home, then starts a journey mid-day, may break their fast that day generates multiple Hanbali views. The rajih position identified by al-Mardawi is that once the fast has been validly started, it must be completed unless there is genuine hardship from continuing — the permission to break is linked to necessity, not to the mere commencement of travel.

Zakat on honey is a distinctive area of Hanbali law: Imam Ahmad — unlike the other major imams — held that zakat is due on honey (one-tenth), based on certain hadiths that other scholars considered weak. Al-Mardawi presents the evidential arguments and the internal Hanbali discussion, noting that the position that zakat is due on honey is the dominant Hanbali view even though it is not shared by the other schools.

For the kaffarah of intentionally breaking the Ramadan fast, the Hanbali school (following the Maliki and Shafi'i schools) holds that the kaffarah applies specifically to breaking the fast through sexual intercourse — not to breaking it through eating or drinking. Al-Mardawi presents this position as the rajih against the Hanafi school's application of kaffarah to all intentional breaks, with careful attention to the evidential basis of both positions.
